text:
Overview
Due: Monday, June 22 by 11:59 PM (Chicago Time)
This first homework assignment has three main goals: to make sure your computer setup works, practice the Git/GitHub workflow, and review the basics of R syntax, Markdown, and R Markdown.
This assignment workflow has several steps by design. All future take-home assignments will use a similar structure, so you will build fluency through repeated practice.


Accessing and Cloning Your hw01 Repository






Important



This will work only if you have submitted your GitHub username (see Lecture 1) and accepted the email invitation to join our GitHub organization (check the email linked to your GitHub account). Make sure you’ve completed the setup under Software Setup and confirmed everything is working properly.


Accessing the repo:

Go at this new link to accept the invitation and create your private hw01 repository on GitHub. Your repository will be named hw01-new-<USERNAME> and will be ready within a few seconds. If this does not work, see the note in red above.
Once the repository has been created, click the link provided to access it.

Cloning the repo into your Workbench:

Log into Workbench and start a new project:
File > New Project > Version Control > Git
In the Repository URL field:

Paste the URL of your GitHub repository. To find the URL, go to your hw01 repo on GitHub, click the green Code button, and copy the SSH or HTTPS link (use the method you configured during setup: Workbench users must select SSH).
The project name should auto-fill. If not, type hw01-new-<USERNAME> manually.

Choose where to save the project directory locally. Tip: Avoid moving folders after cloning them.
Check the box “Open in new session” (recommended for keeping projects separate and organized)
Click Create Project. This will:

Create a new folder on your machine

Initialize a Git repository linked to GitHub

Open an RStudio Project in a new session


You’ll use this same process to access and set up each homework assignment throughout the course.


General Homework Workflow
Your workflow for all homework assignments will follow these steps:

Accept the GitHub repo invitation and clone the repo (see above)
Edit the files included in the repo
Save your changes locally in RStudio Workbench
Pull – Stage – Commit – Push:

Pull from GitHub: this should not change anything if you’re the only one working on the repo, but it’s a good habit to always pull first
Stage and commit your changes to your local Git repository
Push your committed changes to your GitHub repository online


For this course, I assume you’ll be using the Git interface built into RStudio. If you haven’t yet, complete the Using Git within RStudio tutorial to get comfortable with the process.






Tip




Commit and push your work often, multiple times per session if possible. Don’t wait until you’ve finished the entire assignment.

Never edit files directly on GitHub. Always work in your local Git repository (e.g., on Workbench) and then push changes to GitHub. Think of your GitHub repo as a live online backup of your local project.





File Types we use for Homework Assigments
All assignments in this course will be completed and submitted using Markdown and R Markdown files (see Lecture 1 slides for details):

Markdown (.md) is a lightweight formatting language used to structure text.

GitHub README files are typically written in Markdown (.md) or plain text (.txt).
In this course, we use Markdown files only for writing README files because GitHub renders them directly in the browser.
You can reference R code in Markdown, but you cannot run and execute it.

R Markdown (.Rmd) is a format that allows you to combine R code, output, and text in one file.

It’s an extension of Markdown designed for dynamic analysis and reproducible reports specifically for R.
You’ll use R Markdown to complete most homework assignments.


In short, for this course:

Use Markdown (.md) only for writing a short summary in the README.md file
Use R Markdown (.Rmd) for completing the bulk of your assignment, e.g., analysis and code-based work



HW1 Description
For this first assignment (HW1), you’ll edit and push the following two files to your GitHub repo: README.md and code.rmd

Edit the README.md file
You will see a file named README.md in your repository.
A README is usually a plain text (.txt) or Markdown (.md) file that provides important information about a project—such as a summary, installation steps, or example output. In this course, you’ll use it to document your work and reflect on your process.
Your Task: edit the README.md file by replacing the placeholder text with a short biography of yourself. To achieve a “pass”, your biography must include the following elements:

At least one header
Some emphasis (italic or bold text)
At least one list
An image (e.g., a photo of yourself or something meaningful to you)

First, upload the image into your homework repo by importing it in Workbench
Second, embed it in the Markdown file using the correct syntax

At least one link (e.g., to a website or profile)

At the end of your README, add a short reflection that includes:

A brief summary of your Git/GitHub workflow for this homework
(Bullet points or a short narrative are both fine.)
Your evaluation of the homework experience
(E.g., something new you learned, what went well, what confused you, etc.)
A list of resources you consulted and an AI narrative on how you used AI to debug (see Syllabus for details)
Include links, tools, names of any collaborators, and AI statement



Edit the code.rmd file
You will see a file named code.rmd in your repository.
Your task: edit the code.rmd file by making the following changes (all must be included for a “pass”):

Add at least two new YAML header options:

Do not delete or modify the existing ones
Example additions: author:, subtitle:, toc: true, etc

Feel free to experiment, see readings and slides from Lecture 1 for ideas

Add at least two R code chunks with a short explanation in plain text (not as code comment):

Your code can be simple (think at the examples from lecture 1), but it must run and produce the correct output
Your final knitted .html .md file should show both code and output (we want the md as Github cannot read html, but if you submit both that’s fine)
If your code generates plots (not required, but possible), make sure these plots are also pushed to GitHub, else they won’t show up there





How to Submit this Assignment
To submit your assignment on Canvas, follow these steps:

Before the deadline, push the final version of your work to your GitHub repository. Tip: don’t wait until the end to make your first commit—commit, stage, and push your work regularly as you go.
Your GitHub repository should include at least these three files:

The updated README.md
The edited code.rmd
A knitted output file that you generated from code.rmd (this must be a .md document, not anything else)

When you’re ready to submit:

Copy the URL of your GitHub repository. It will look something like: https://github.com/css-summer26/hw01-new-yourusername
Submit that URL on Canvas under HW01. Do not upload any files to Canvas. We only need the repository link.




Assessment
This assignment is evaluated on a Pass/Fail basis. To earn a Pass:
Your README.md should include a clear introduction that meets all specified requirements. It should demonstrate your ability to use Markdown syntax, such as headers, emphasis, lists, links, and images. You should also include a short description of how you edited and committed your changes, along with reflections on your experience using Git/GitHub, Markdown, and R Markdown.
Your code.rmd file must knit successfully. It should contain at least two new YAML header fields and two R code chunks, each introduced with a brief explanation written as plain text (not as code comments).
All required files must be pushed to your GitHub repository (see above). If you have graphs, ensure they are visible in your knitted .md document
For more details, see the Assessment section of the Syllabus.

You are more than welcome to touch base with us if you are not sure or want to brainstorm your ideas with us.

Click Create Project. This will:

Initialize a Git repository linked to GitHub

Open an RStudio Project in a new session








Note



The repo you clone for the final project is intentionally empty: you will need to add your own data and code, and push them. See the instructions below for what to submit. Remember to include the knitted .md file(s)!.




General Homework Workflow
See Homework 1 for details. The workflow is the same for all assignments (e.g., accept the repo, edit the files, save, then Pull – Stage – Commit – Push).


Assignment Description

1. Assigment Goals
In this assignment, you have the opportunity to apply the skills learned in the course to a research question of your choice. Your task is to find data, ask a research question, and answer it descriptively using data and code. Your main deliverable is a report with your code, graphs/tables, and interpretation.
The sections below walk you through the details of each part of the assignment.


2. Report Instructions

Format and Target

Format: the report must be written using a .Rmd file, and knitted to.md and to .pdf
The report must include:

Written text
Code chunks
Code outputs (tables, plots, descriptive stats)
Not all code you write is required to go in your report: you may place extra code in a separate R script or Rmd. If you do so, mention this in your README.md.

Audience: Write your report as if you were preparing a polished analysis for either an academic audience or a professional industry setting.
Length: Aim for ~800 words (minimum 600, maximum 1000).
Use of R Markdown Syntax: Use clear headings and subheadings and include a table of contents. If feasible, try to include some of the advanced R Markdown from the last lecture of the course.



Required Sections
Specifically, your report should include the following sections:

Project description

Start by explaining what your project is about, its goals, and why it matters.
Describe the dataset (number of observations, time period, key variables, data source). Store the data in your repo, if smaller than 100 MB; if larger, see below.
State one or more questions, ideally with social science relevance, and your hypothesis or describe what you expect to find and why.

Descriptive statistics and plots

Include tables (format them with kable) and descriptive statistics (counts, percentages, contingency tables, etc.).
Include 4 or 5 well-designed plots with clear titles, labels, scales, etc. Use different types of plots (do not use the same plot more than once) and ensure each one matches the underlying variable types.
Use code to transform or prepare variables for your plots and analyses when needed (e.g., aggregating, recoding, standardizing, creating new variables, etc.).
Describe the main findings of each graph, highlight patterns, discuss possible relationships between variables, note limitations, and suggest hypotheses or directions for future research. We want to hear your explanation and interpretation of the analyses!

Modeling is optional

You are not expected or required to include statistical models since we did not cover them in the course. Excellent projects can rely entirely on descriptive statistics and the types of plots in HW2 and HW3.
If you choose to add models (e.g., regression, ANOVA, text analysis, etc.), make sure to use them appropriately: check assumptions, interpret coefficients, and ensure the method fits your variables and research question. If you include a model, it will be evaluated for the insights it provides.





3. Code Instructions
This course has a strong coding component, so the quality and organization of your code are an important part of the project. Specifically:

Library Loading and Data Import

Load all required libraries and import your data once at the top of your Rmd. Do not reload libraries elsewhere.




Data Cleaning, Wrangling, and Tidying

Include code needed to prepare your data (renaming, recoding, aggregating, reshaping, tidying data, joining data etc.). The amount of cleaning will depend on your project.
If your code for data prep and cleaning is extensive, place it in a separate script or Rmd file (see below).

Data Analysis and Plots

Include code for descriptive statistics (tables, summaries, counts, percentages, contingency tables, etc.).
Include code for 4–5 well-designed plots. Use a variety of plot types and match each to the variable types. If your dataset requires little cleaning, we expect more complex and refined visualizations.

Programming Techniques

Include at least one user-defined function AND either a loop (or a map/across/apply alternative) OR a conditional statement. If these don’t naturally fit your project, check in with us and we’ll help you find the best approach.

Code Organization

Include only relevant output (e.g., show the head of your dataset, not the full dataset) and follow coding practices from class: clean style, reproducible workflow, and Markdown formatting. Prefer tidyverse functions over base R, where applicable (e.g., use read_csv() instead of read.csv()).

Extensive cleaning steps or extra plots can go in a separate script or Rmd. If you do so, in the main report, load the cleaned data and include only the required plots. Store all code in your repository to ensure reproducibility. Let us know the location of any additional code in your README.md.




4. Repository and README.md Instructions
Your repository must include a README.md with the following information:

Purpose of the repository: A bullet point explanation of what the project is about (e.g., topic, data source, sample size).
List of files: A list of all R scripts and .Rmd files in the repo, each with a one-sentence description.
Required packages: Any non-basic packages needed to run your code (no need to list tidyverse or rmarkdown).
Additional information: Anything else a user needs to understand or replicate your work.

How do I know if I have “done enough”?

First, make sure you’ve met all requirements for the report, code, and README.md.
As a rough benchmark, this assignment should take about as much time as an average previous one. It’s worth 20 points instead of 15, since finding data and developing your own question typically require a bit more work. Please take this only as a guideline, actual time will vary by student, project, and working style.
Aim to review what you’ve learned and push a bit beyond your comfort zone. If you’re unsure whether your plan is sufficient, check in with us.
This assignment should reflect your own work. You may seek advice by humans or robots, but your code must be written by you, understandable to you, and aligned with our AI policy.



What data should I use?
We encourage you to use social science data; other types of data can work too but check with us first. What matters most is applying the skills you learned to a topic that interests you: the best projects are the ones you are genuinely excited to work on.


I can’t think of anything to analyze!
You may use any dataset from class not used in previous homework and not tied to teaching examples (e.g., penguins, cars, iris datasets are not appropriate). Some options to consider:

gun_deaths from library(rcis), or the raw data from FiveThirtyEight (it’s a similar topic but this is different dataset than HW2)
scorecard from library(rcis) or use the rscorecard package to download your own subset
Explore the Data Is Plural dataset archive
Explore the #TidyTuesday dataset archive



What if my data file is too large?
GitHub does not allow files larger than 100MB. If you try to push one, you’ll get an error and may have to remove the file from your Git history, which is a hassle. Thus:

If your data is well under 100MB, include it in your repo.

If it is close to or above 100MB, do not include it in your repo. Instead, store it outside GitHub (e.g., Google Drive) and include a link to it in your README.md.
